topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias leaned back in his worn desk chair, a slow, deliberate smile spreading across his face. The printed essay, complete with the glossy university logo and the perfectly curated anecdote about his volunteer work, lay open before him. He traced the elegant script of the opening paragraph with a fingertip, a faint, satisfied hum vibrating through his chest. He’d spent three sleepless nights agonizing over his own narrative, wrestling with the perfect phrasing, and now, here it was—a flawless blueprint. He tapped the screen once, a light, almost dismissive sound. "Well," he murmured to the empty room, his voice carrying a tone of quiet triumph, "that certainly sets a high bar." He folded the paper neatly, tucking it into a drawer as if cataloging a prized artifact.

[story 2]

Maya picked up the printed submission, her eyes scanning the meticulously crafted prose. A small, self-assured curve lifted the corner of her mouth. She ran a hand through her hair, a gesture that conveyed a deep sense of self-possession. She reread the description of her intellectual curiosity, feeling a distinct warmth bloom in her cheeks. It was undeniably excellent; every sentence felt sharp and perfectly placed. She didn't need to rewrite anything. Instead, she placed the document carefully on the corner of her monitor, her gaze lingering on the polished words. A slight nod, almost imperceptible, acknowledged the sheer quality of the work, a silent declaration that she understood exactly what it took to succeed.

[story 3]

When Liam finally spotted the published essay, he didn't immediately move. He just stared, his jaw tightening almost imperceptibly. A slow, almost imperceptible relaxation followed, and a distinct air of superiority settled around him. He leaned forward, his posture straightening, as if the very act of observing the perfect execution was a privilege he had earned. "Remarkable," he breathed out, the word laced with an undeniable sense of having already surpassed the standard. He picked up a pen, not to edit, but to underline a particularly strong sentence, his movements precise and utterly confident. He felt a distinct sense of having already mastered the assignment.
